questions.question: Explain the Waterfall Model with its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Waterfall is a sequential model with phases: Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, Maintenance
Advantages: Simple to understand, clear milestones, well-documented phases, suitable for stable requirements
Disadvantages: Inflexible to changes, high risk, late testing, customer sees product only at end

questions.question: Differentiate between Verification and Validation with examples.
keyPoints: Verification: Checking if product is built correctly (Are we building the product right?)
Validation: Checking if right product is built (Are we building the right product?)
Example: Verification - reviewing design documents; Validation - user acceptance testing

questions.question: Describe the Spiral Model and explain its four main activities in each iteration.
keyPoints: Spiral model combines iterative development with systematic risk assessment
Four activities: Planning (define objectives), Risk Analysis (identify and mitigate risks), Engineering (develop and test), Evaluation (assess results and plan next iteration)
Each cycle produces an improved version of the software

questions.question: List and briefly explain the four values of the Agile Manifesto.
keyPoints: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
Working software over comprehensive documentation
Customer collaboration over contract negotiation
Responding to change over following a plan
These values emphasize flexibility, customer satisfaction, and iterative delivery

questions.question: What is the V-Model? Explain how verification and validation activities are mapped in the V-Model.
keyPoints: V-Model is an extension of waterfall with simultaneous development and testing
Left side: Development phases (Requirements → Detailed Design → Implementation)
Right side: Testing phases (Unit Testing → Integration → System → Acceptance)
Each development phase has corresponding testing phase below it in the V shape

questions.question: Explain the Scrum framework with its roles and ceremonies.
keyPoints: Scrum is an Agile framework for iterative development
Three roles: Product Owner (defines priorities), Scrum Master (facilitates), Development Team (delivers)
Ceremonies: Sprint Planning, Daily Stand-up, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective
Artifacts: Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, Increment

questions.question: What is CMMI? Briefly explain the five maturity levels.
keyPoints: CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration) is a process improvement framework
Level 1: Initial (process is ad-hoc, chaotic)
Level 2: Managed (basic project management processes exist)
Level 3: Defined (processes are well-defined and standardized)
Level 4: Quantitatively Managed (quantitative performance management)
Level 5: Optimizing (continuous process improvement)

questions.question: Discuss the Spiral Model in detail. How does it address risk management? Explain with a diagram showing the four quadrants of each spiral iteration.
keyPoints: Spiral model combines iterative development with risk assessment
Iterative nature allows for repeated cycles through four quadrants:
1. Planning: Define objectives, constraints, risk identification
2. Risk Analysis: Identify alternatives, assess and mitigate risks
3. Engineering: Develop, test, integrate the product
4. Evaluation: Review results, plan next iteration
Advantages: Handles high-risk projects, flexible, early customer feedback, incremental delivery
Disadvantages: Complex, requires expertise in risk analysis, may not suit small projects
Risk management is central: each iteration identifies risks, develops strategies to mitigate them before moving forward, reducing project failure probability

questions.question: Compare and contrast at least four software process models (Waterfall, Incremental, Spiral, V-Model) with respect to their suitability, advantages, and limitations.
keyPoints: Waterfall: Sequential, simple, good for stable requirements but inflexible to changes
Incremental: Delivers in small increments, reduces risk, flexible but may need refactoring
Spiral: Risk-driven, flexible, good for complex projects but complex to manage
V-Model: Testing integrated early, good for safety-critical systems but rigid
Comparison table showing: development approach, risk handling, user involvement, flexibility, testing integration
Selection criteria depends on project size, requirements stability, risk level, timeline

questions.question: Explain the Agile Software Development methodology. Discuss the Scrum process in detail including roles, artifacts, and ceremonies.
keyPoints: Agile: Iterative approach emphasizing flexibility, customer collaboration, working software
Values from Manifesto: individuals over interactions, working software over docs, collaboration over contracts, responding to change over plans
Scrum Details:
Roles: Product Owner (what to build), Scrum Master (how to build), Team (build it)
Artifacts: Product Backlog (what needs done), Sprint Backlog (current sprint tasks), Increment (potentially shippable product)
Ceremonies: Sprint Planning (plan work), Daily Scrum (15-min sync), Sprint Review (demo), Sprint Retrospective (improve process)
Sprint: 2-4 week time-boxed iteration producing working increment

questions.question: Explain the concept of software process improvement. Discuss the CMMI model and its importance in improving software quality.
keyPoints: Software Process Improvement (SPI) aims to make software development more efficient and predictable
CMMI is a well-known framework for SPI
Five Maturity Levels:
- Level 1: Initial (chaotic)
- Level 2: Managed (basic processes)
- Level 3: Defined (standardized)
- Level 4: Quantitatively Managed (measured)
- Level 5: Optimizing (continuously improving)
Benefits: Better quality, reduced costs, improved schedules, higher customer satisfaction
Implementation involves assessing current processes, identifying gaps, implementing improvements, measuring results
